title: "null: Permission denied" after "mknod -m 0666 null c 1 3" in a fully privileged container
body:
### Issue Description

```
❯ sudo podman run --rm -it --privileged tumbleweed
a6a360335ceb:/ # mkdir /root/test
a6a360335ceb:/ # mknod -m 0666 /root/test/null c 1 3
a6a360335ceb:/ # ls -la /root/test/
total 8
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Jun 12 08:19 .
drwx------ 1 root root 4096 Jun 12 08:19 ..
crw-rw-rw- 1 root root 1, 3 Jun 12 08:19 null
a6a360335ceb:/ # echo 1 > /root/test/null
bash: /root/test/null: Permission denied
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Start a container with `sudo` and `--privileged`
2. create a node for /dev/null with  `mknod -m 0666 /path/null c 1 3`
3. Try to write to that node, e.g. `echo 1 > /path/null`



### Describe the results you received

Observe `Permission denied` error

### Describe the results you expected

No errors

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

regular desktop

### Additional information

problem doesn't happen in mapped volumes

comments.body:
this worked for me here using the main branch.

```
$ sudo podman run --rm -it --privileged tumbleweed
Resolved "tumbleweed" as an alias (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/000-shortnames.conf)
Trying to pull registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/tumbleweed:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob 446e6e3b80bf done   | 
Copying config 8847b24137 done   | 
Writing manifest to image destination
0b1baeed9982:/ # mkdir /root/test
0b1baeed9982:/ # mknod -m 0666 /root/test/null c 1 3
0b1baeed9982:/ # ls -la /root/test/
total 0
drwxr-xr-x. 1 root root    8 Jun 12 14:12 .
drwx------. 1 root root    8 Jun 12 14:12 ..
crw-rw-rw-. 1 root root 1, 3 Jun 12 14:12 null
0b1baeed9982:/ # echo 1 > /root/test/null
0b1baeed9982:/ # 
```

> this worked for me here using the main branch

Which kernel is on your host? Mine is tumbleweed host with 6.15 kernel
btw in case if it is relevant: the commands work in docker with and without sudo and --privileged variants.
Any chance this is an SELinux denial?

> Any chance this is an SELinux denial?

I don’t think so, I could reproduce https://github.com/openSUSE/osc/issues/1795 even with clean `ausearch -m AVC`.

with help of @dcermak I confirm it is `runc` - related:
```
❯ sudo podman run --rm -it --privileged --runtime /usr/bin/crun tumbleweed
[sudo] password for root: 
8c52e9a47c59:/ # 
8c52e9a47c59:/ # mkdir /root/test ; mknod -m 0666 /root/test/null c 1 3 ; echo 1 > /root/test/null
8c52e9a47c59:/ # exit
exit
❯ sudo podman run --rm -it --privileged tumbleweed
5d327916d511:/ # mkdir /root/test ; mknod -m 0666 /root/test/null c 1 3 ; echo 1 > /root/test/null
bash: /root/test/null: Permission denied

```

It still might be podman-specific problem(?) because docker works properly with runc (and doesn't need any privileges):
```
❯ docker run --rm -it --runtime runc opensuse/tumbleweed
cf21ee79b782:/ # mkdir /root/test ; mknod -m 0666 /root/test/null c 1 3 ; echo 1 > /root/test/null
cf21ee79b782:/ # exit
exit
```
why not use crun? its the default for us upstream, in fedora, and rhel.  or is this during a testing run of runc you hit this?
(I have SELinux disabled, but have apparmor. Still nothing pops up in the audit log when the problem command is executed)

> why not use crun? its the default for us upstream, in fedora, and rhel. or is this during a testing run of runc you hit this?

I guess runc is default in openSUSE Tumbleweed and MicroOS. Yes, we are still considering possible workarounds.

docker adds CAP_MKNOD, while Podman doesn't.  You don't need a fully privileged container to allow `mknod`, only `--cap-add mknod`.

I've not been able to reproduce on Fedora, tried both crun and runc.

@giuseppe actually interesting question, not sure why [this comment](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/4622/files) claims only root containers may work and adding CAP_MKNOD will not help; while docker still can do it. On which kernel is your fedora, maybe it is related somehow?

```
> podman run --cap-add mknod --rm --runtime /usr/bin/runc --privileged tumbleweed bash -c "mkdir /root/test ; mknod -m 0666 /root/test/null c 1 3 ; echo 1 > /root/test/null;"
mknod: /root/test/null: Operation not permitte
```

CAP_MKNOD works only for root, when you are in a user namespace you are still limited (only c 0 0 can be created, but that is the overlay whiteout file).

I am on 6.14.9-300.fc42.x86_64
with Docker you connect to the daemon that runs as root, so you are not really doing any unprivileged operation in a user namespace.
